title: Preparing for the shearing contractor
subtitle: What to do the day before and on shearing day to make the job go smoothly for everyone.
excerpt: A shearing contractor's day is measured in animals per hour. Sheep that are clean, dry, empty, and ready to handle shear faster, produce a better fleece, and reduce the chance of a rescheduled visit. Most of the preparation happens in the 24 to 48 hours before the contractor arrives and takes little time if planned properly.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef and Lamb: sheep shearing husbandry guide (2022), ahdb.org.uk. National Sheep Association: working with a contractor, nationalsheep.org.uk. John Seymour, The New Complete Book of Self-Sufficiency, shearing chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: The natural separation that develops between the old fleece and the new wool growth in late spring, typically in April or May in the UK. The rise makes it easier for the blades or handpiece to run smoothly between the skin and the fleece. Shearing before the rise is harder for the shearer and more likely to cause skin nicks.

Removing soiled wool around the dock and hindquarters before shearing. Wool matted with faeces clogs shearing blades and adds weight and contamination to the shorn fleece. Dagging before the contractor arrives is considered basic preparation courtesy.

The process of removing the heavily stained, daggy, and short-staple edges of a shorn fleece before it is rolled or bagged. Skirted fleeces fetch better prices at auction and are easier to process for hand spinners.

content.text: Shearing day involves someone else's time and equipment, which changes the dynamic compared to most solo smallholding tasks. The contractor arrives with a specific number of animals to cover before the next farm. The preparation you do beforehand determines whether that visit runs on schedule or becomes difficult for both parties.
Timing: wait for the rise
Book your shearing date after the
shearing rise

has developed, usually late April to early June depending on breed and region. Early lambing breeds with a short fleece (Southdown, Herdwick) are sometimes done in May; longwool breeds in June. Shearing before the rise is harder work for the contractor and produces more second cuts in the fleece.
Forty-eight hours before
Complete any
dagging
that has not been done recently. Matted dags at the hindquarters slow the shearer significantly. Use hand shears or dagging shears.
Check the forecast: sheep shear poorly when wet and a wet fleece will be refused by most contractors or left on the animal. Pen the flock under a covered yard the night before if rain is forecast.
Withdraw feed the evening before: empty guts are safer for sheep on their backs and make handling easier for the shearer. Water should remain available.
On shearing day

Pen sheep in a small holding pen near the shearing station. The contractor needs a steady flow of animals that can be caught easily. A draughty field pen where sheep scatter is frustrating and slow; a solid-sided pen with one exit to the shearing position is much better.

The shearing position itself needs a clean, solid floor with good grip for the shearer's feet. A piece of clean board or ply on concrete is ideal. Keep the area clear of muck, loose hay, and trip hazards.
Rolling and bagging the fleece
Have a sheet of clean board or canvas on the floor beside the shearing position for the shorn fleece to fall onto. While the contractor moves to the next sheep,
skirt

the fleece by removing stained and daggy edges, then roll it with the cut side out. The rolled fleece goes directly into a clean hessian or polypropylene wool bag. Do not use string or twine that sheds fibres; they contaminate the fleece and are extremely difficult to remove at processing.
After shearing

Freshly shorn sheep are vulnerable to cold rain for 2 to 3 weeks after shearing. Keep them in a covered pen or under a shelter if wet weather follows the shearing date. A shorn sheep in early spring can lose condition rapidly if caught in cold rain overnight.

Check each animal for skin nicks. The contractor's blades are sharp and occasionally catch a fold of skin, particularly around the brisket or inside the leg. A small application of wound spray on any cut that has drawn blood prevents fly strike developing on the exposed area before the fleece regrows.
